skill.name: json-transformer
skill.description: Transform, validate, query, compare, and convert JSON data using Python, JavaScript, jq, and schema-based workflows.
skill.summary: Practical recipes for JSON validation, transformation, extraction, conversion, diffing, and schema generation.
skill.author: CuriousLearner

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found most were Markdown examples, ordinary JSON file I/O, or keyword false positives. The skill is not blocked because no malicious intent, prompt injection, network exfiltration, or secret access was confirmed. Publication should include a medium warning because it teaches command-line and file-writing workflows that users may copy into local environments.

medium_findings.title: Executable Command Examples Require Review
medium_findings.description: The skill includes jq and Python command-line examples that read JSON files, transform data, and redirect output. These are legitimate JSON workflows, but users should review paths and inputs before running copied commands.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Direct command examples are present, but they are documentation snippets and are not executed by the skill itself. The risk depends on a user copying commands against untrusted files or paths.

medium_findings.title: Configuration Merge Example Accepts User Data
medium_findings.description: The configuration merge example combines a base configuration with user configuration and then checks required fields. If copied into production without allowlists or conflict handling, untrusted input could override sensitive settings.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The deep merge pattern is visible and uses user_config, but the snippet is illustrative and no implementation of deep_merge is provided. This is a moderate guidance risk rather than active malicious code.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Misclassified as Ruby Backticks
low_findings.description: Many external command findings point to Markdown code fences around examples, not Ruby shell backtick execution. These locations do not show automatic command execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are Markdown fences or language labels surrounding examples. I found no Ruby backtick expression or automatic execution at these locations.

low_findings.title: Filesystem Examples Are Ordinary JSON Input and Output
low_findings.description: The file operations are examples for reading JSON files and writing transformed output files. They do not contain path traversal, deletion, persistence, or hidden data collection behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The snippets use standard read and write APIs with fixed example filenames. They are still relevant to user data handling, but no destructive or covert behavior is evident.

low_findings.title: Sensitive Keyword Detections Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The high findings for certificate or key material point to ordinary terms such as all_keys, key transformation, and an example api_key field name. No secret value, certificate file, or credential exfiltration was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited code uses generic JSON object keys and a required field name. There is no access to secret storage, environment variables, or network transmission.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Attempt Found
low_findings.description: A targeted review found no text that claims higher authority, tells the auditor to ignore instructions, or attempts to override security analysis.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The introduction and instructions describe the JSON transformer role without suspicious override language. A targeted search for common injection phrases returned no evidence.

dangerous_patterns.title: Executable Command Examples Require Review
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes jq and Python command-line examples that read JSON files, transform data, and redirect output. These are legitimate JSON workflows, but users should review paths and inputs before running copied commands.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Direct command examples are present, but they are documentation snippets and are not executed by the skill itself. The risk depends on a user copying commands against untrusted files or paths.

dangerous_patterns.title: Configuration Merge Example Accepts User Data
dangerous_patterns.description: The configuration merge example combines a base configuration with user configuration and then checks required fields. If copied into production without allowlists or conflict handling, untrusted input could override sensitive settings.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The deep merge pattern is visible and uses user_config, but the snippet is illustrative and no implementation of deep_merge is provided. This is a moderate guidance risk rather than active malicious code.

content.value_statement: JSON work often spans validation, reshaping, querying, and format conversion. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical recipes for common JSON transformation tasks.
actual_capabilities: Parse, validate, pretty-print, and compact JSON data.
Extract values with JSONPath, JMESPath, jq, and safe access helpers.
Flatten, unflatten, merge, filter, map, sort, and deduplicate JSON structures.
Convert JSON to CSV, YAML, and XML using Python and command-line examples.
Compare JSON documents and generate basic schemas from example data.
Restructure API responses and configuration data into application-friendly shapes.
limitations: The skill is documentation only and does not include executable helper scripts.
Examples use small sample data and may need changes for large files or streaming workloads.
Command-line snippets can overwrite output files if users reuse the same filenames.
Schema generation examples infer basic shapes and do not replace careful contract design.

use_cases.title: Clean API Responses
use_cases.description: Reshape nested API payloads into stable fields for application code, reports, or downstream tools.

use_cases.title: Prepare Data Exports
use_cases.description: Flatten nested JSON and convert selected fields into CSV, YAML, or XML for sharing and analysis.

use_cases.title: Validate JSON Contracts
use_cases.description: Check JSON documents against schemas and identify clear validation errors before integration work.

prompt_templates.title: Format and Validate JSON
prompt_templates.prompt: Use json-transformer to validate this JSON, explain any errors in plain language, and show a clean formatted version.

prompt_templates.title: Extract Nested Fields
prompt_templates.prompt: Use json-transformer to extract the user name, email, status, and most recent activity date from this nested JSON payload.

prompt_templates.title: Convert JSON for Analysis
prompt_templates.prompt: Use json-transformer to flatten this array of nested records and describe how to export the selected fields as CSV.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Transformation Pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Use json-transformer to design a safe transformation pipeline that validates input, normalizes keys, merges defaults, and reports changed fields.

output_examples.input: A nested customer API response with profile, address, and account status fields.
output_examples.output: A concise mapping plan that extracts customer identity, contact fields, normalized status, and pagination details.

output_examples.input: A list of JSON records that need to become a spreadsheet-friendly export.
output: A conversion approach for CSV output

output_examples.input: Two versions of a JSON configuration document.
output_examples.output: A change summary that separates added fields, removed fields, changed values, and type changes.

best_practices: Validate input before transforming it, especially when data comes from users or external systems.
Write transformed output to a new file name until the result has been reviewed.
Use explicit field mappings when converting nested JSON into flat tables.
anti_patterns: Do not run copied shell commands on sensitive directories without checking file paths first.
Do not deep merge untrusted configuration without allowlists and conflict rules.
Do not infer production schemas from one small sample without reviewing optional fields.

faq.question: Does this skill include runnable code?
faq.answer: No. It is a Markdown skill with examples and recipes that an assistant can adapt for JSON tasks.

faq.question: Can it validate JSON Schema documents?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes examples using Python jsonschema and JavaScript Ajv for schema validation.

faq.question: Can it convert JSON to CSV?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers pandas, the Python csv module, and jq approaches for CSV conversion.

faq.question: Can it work with nested JSON?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes flattening, unflattening, path extraction, and safe nested access patterns.

faq.question: Does it access environment variables or secrets?
faq.answer: No evidence found. The audit found an api_key field name in an example, not secret access.

faq.question: What should users review before running examples?
faq.answer: Users should review input paths, output paths, redirects, and merge behavior before running command-line examples.
